details.text: Derivative: فُصِّلَتْ is the passive form from فَصَّلَ – تَفْصِيل which means here “to explain in detail”. It comes from the meaning of فَصَّلَ “to separate” or “to divide” in the case where the parts are separated and explained to provide clarity.

Derivative: أُحْكِمَتْ is the passive form from أَحْكَمَ – إِحْكَام which can mean to be precise, concise and perfected in a way that it is protected from corruption. It can also imply to be full of wisdom and rulings.

Derivative: حَصِيدٌ in origin refers to the harvest of a crop and its yield. It is used to depict what is left of the land after the crop has been harvested in terms of a barren land with just stubble remaining. It is being used as a metaphor to mean that there is nothing remaining.
Derivative: تَتْبِيب (Tatbeeb) is the تَفْعِيل (Taf’eel) form from تَّبَّ (Tabba) which means to be destroyed and ruined. تَتْبِيب (Tatbeeb) means ruin and destruction.

Derivative: تَكَلَّمُ is in origin تَتَكَلَّمُ but the first تاء (Taa’) has been omitted. The lessening of the word in Arabic can imply a lessening of the meaning. As such, “to speak” can mean “to speak a little” or in its context here “A Nafs (person) will not even speak a little”.

Derivative: زَفِيرٌ (Zafeer) is used to refer to the initial sound that a donkey makes when it exhales out of exhaustion and is generally used to describe the process of exhaling and the sound that someone makes when they are exhausted.
Derivative: شَهِيقٌ (Shaheeq)

- When Phrase: زَفِيرٌ وَشَهِيقٌ (Zafeer and Shaheeq) in general refer to an animal’s loud or uncomfortable inhaling and exhaling when it is being burdened. Here it can be a description of the sound of hellfire or the sounds made by the people themselves due to what they will be experienced in hell.

Derivative: مَجْذُوذٍ (Majdhoodh) comes from جَذَّ which means to cut off or clip something. مَجْذُوذٍ (Majdhoodh) means cut off or interrupted. غَيْرَ مَجْذُوذٍ (Ghaira Majdhoodh) means to not be interrupted or cut off i.e., unceasing.
Derivative: تَرْكَنُوا comes from رَكَنَ – يَرْكُنُ – رُكُون which means to lean on something for support or to incline towards something and have reliance upon it.

Derivative: أُتْرِفُوا is in the passive form from أَتْرَفَ – يُتْرِفُ – إِتْرَاف which means “to be provide with luxury” or “to surround in luxury”. It is most commonly used in the passive to refer to what “has been given in terms of luxury”. مُتْرَف (Mutraf) is the one who enjoys luxury and a life of ease.

- “Those possessing (أُولُو) Baqiyyah (بَقِيَّةٍ)” – بَقِيَّةٍ (Baqiyyah) refers to noble traits and qualities that people hold onto. The origin of بَقِيَّةٍ (Baqiyyah) is what a person chooses for himself in terms of precious things that he saves or holds onto to benefit by them later. أُولُو بَقِيَّةٍ (Uoloo Baqiyyah) refers to the people who remained and held onto upon noble values and good qualities.

Derivative: مُصْلِحُونَ comes from أًصْلَحَ – يُصْلِحُ – إِصْلاح and it differs from صَالِحُونَ which means to act righteously in that the مُصْلِحُ (Muslih) refers to the one who spreads righteousness to others in the society.

Derivative: يُبْخَسُونَ is the passive form from بَخَسَ – يَبْخُسُ – بَخْس which means to replace something with something inferior or to wrongfully give less than what is rightly due to someone i.e., to shortchange him. لَا يُبْخَسُونَmeans “They will not be given less than what they are rightfully due”.

Derivative: صَنَعُوا comes from صَنَعَ which means “to manufacture” and is used to mean “to do” whilst implying what you have done has taken some time and there has been some process to it, like the meaning of “what you have worked for” in your life.

- لَا جَرَمَ أَنَّ (Laa Jarama Anna) is an Arabic expression to indicate to something that is bound and inevitable to happen whilst also carrying the implication that there are no obstacles in the way and that nothing will stop it. It can be generally understood by meanings such as “Truly”, “Surely”, “Undoubtedly”, “Inevitably” and other similar meanings.

Derivative: أَخْبَتُوا comes from أَخْبَتَ – يُخْبِتُ – إِخْبات which is used to mean to turn to Allah in a state of peace and tranquillity. It includes the meaning of being humble and submissive to Allah.

Derivative: الْمَلَأُ (Mala’) comes from مَلَأَ – يَمْلَأُ – مَلْئًا which means to fill. It refers to those who fill the influential and powerful positions and circles in a society and also those who are filled themselves in terms of their own power and influence. It refers to those of power, leadership, influence and prominence within a society.

Derivative: أَرَاذِل (Araadhil) is the plural of أَرْذَل (Ardhal) which is the اِسم تَفْضِيل (Ism Tafdeel - superlative) of رَذِيل (Radheel) which means “lowly, vile or contemptible”. أَرْذَل (Ardhal) means “The lowest”, “Most contemptible” or “Vilest”. أَرَاذِل (Araadhil) are those are regarded to have the lowest status within society.

- بَادِيَ الرَّأْيِ is an expression which means to come to a quick conclusion about something based on what is immediately apparent to you without engaging in deep thought or analysis. In this context it means that “They followed you on face value without applying any deep thought or consideration” i.e., they were quick to follow.
Derivative: طَارِد is the active participle (الاِسْمُ الفاعِل – Al-Ismu l-Faa’il) from the verb طَرَدَ – يَطْرُدُ – طَرْد and it means to push, drive away, banish or expel.
Derivative: تَوَلَّوْا is in origin تَتَوَلُّوا but one of the Taa’s has been omitted. It means “You (plural) turn away”.

Derivative: تَزْدَرِي comes from اِزْدَرَى – يَزْدِرِي – اِزْدِرَاءً which is from the root letters زرى which means to think of somebody as being worthless, to think little of them and look down upon them with contempt.
Derivative: يُغْوِيَ is from أَغْوَى which means to make somebody veer off a path where they are unable to find their way back i.e., to lead astray and mislead.

Derivative: نُصْح (Nus’h) is from نَصَحَ – يَنْصَحُ which means to give sincere advice. نُصْح (Nus’h) refers to the sincere advice that is given for the betterment of somebody without being stained by one’s ego.

Derivative: إِجْرَام (Ijraam) comes from أَجْرَمَ – يُجْرِمُ - إِجْرامًا which means to perpetrate a crime. إِجْرَام (Ijraam) refers to a “crime” or “criminality” deserving of a penalty or punishment. It can refer here to “crime”, “sin” or “the evil consequences of the crime/sin”.

Derivative: comes from اِبْتَأَسَ - يَبْتَئِسُ – اِبْتِئَاسًا which means to be distressed and to grieve. It comes from بَأْس (Ba’s) according to its meaning of “hurt, injury and impairment”. When such discomfort is experienced the word اِبْتَأَسَ is used to express the grief and distress.

Derivative: مُّغْرَقُونَ is the plural of مُغْرَق (Mughraq). It is the الاِسْمُ المَفْعُول (Al-Ismu l-Maf’ool – Passive participle) and means “Drowned” implying that it’s a done deal i.e., although they are still alive now, they are in reality already dead and destroyed”.
Derivative: مُّقِيمٌ (Muqeem) can mean “lasting” and “permanent” which is its meaning here in respect to the punishment of the hereafter.
Derivative: فَارَ – يَفُورُ – فَوْرًا means to rise up and boil over.
Derivative: مَجْرَى (Majraa) is a Masdar (Verbal noun) from جَرَى – يَجْرِي – جَرْي which means “to run” and “to flow”. مَجْرَى (Majraa) here refers to “the course” that the ship will take.

Derivative: مَعْزِلٍ (Ma’zil) comes from عَزَلَ – يَعْزِلُ – عَزْل which means to “isolate”, “separate” or “detach”. مَعْزِلٍ (Ma’zil) refers to a place of isolation or detachment. The meaning here is that the son of Nuh (as) was alone in a place detached and separated from him and the believers on the boat.
Derivative: أَقْلِعِي is the command form from أَقْلَعَ – إِقْلاع which means “to halt or desist” from doing something after being engaged and busily involved in it.
Derivative: غِيضَ is the passive form from غَاضَ – يَغِيضُ – غَيْض which means to decrease and diminish gradually i.e., to subside, recede and dwindle away.
- بُعْدًا لِّ “Bu’dan Li” means “Away with or be gone with” which implies that they have been finished and destroyed. It is a statement that carries no regret like “good riddance”.

- يَا أَرْضُ (Yaa Ardu) “O earth”. Here the earth is being addressed with يَا “Yaa” directly unlike the address to humans in the Qur’an which is يَا أَيُّهَا “Yaa Ayyuhaa” in the case where the أَيُّهَا (Ayyuhaa) is for the purpose of grabbing attention. This indicates that the earth does not require its attention to be grabbed and is in a constant state of submission and always ready to fulfil the command of its Rabb whilst the human is not always paying attention and is heedless of his Rabb (Lord and Master). The same applies to His statement: يَا سَمَاءُ (Yaa Samaa’u) “O sky” mentioned in the same Aayah.
Derivative: يَثْنُونَ comes from ثَنَى – يَثْنِي – ثَنْي and its meaning here is “to cover” or “to fold over or up”.

- “There is not (مَا) for you in terms of (لَكُم مِّنْ) an Ilaah (إِلَٰهٍ) other than Him (غَيْرُهُ)” - مَا “Maa” is used for the purpose of negation and therefore implies the existence of an opposing argument. It is unlike the statementلَا إِلَٰهٍ “Laa Ilaaha” which is a statement of fact.
Derivative: مِّدْرَارًا refers to rain that is abundant. It comes from the word دَرَّ which means to flow abundantly or to be abundant and plentiful.
Derivative: اعْتَرَى means to be afflicted or seized by something like a sickness that takes away your mind. It is also used to mean “to take possession” of someone.
Derivative: يَسْتَخْلِفُ comes from اِسْتَخْلَفَ – اسْتِخْلَاف which means to make a successor or inheritor i.e., someone who comes after you or in your place.

Derivative: عَنِيدٍ (‘Aneed) comes from the verb عَنِدَ – يَعْنَدُ – عَنَاد which means to be stubborn and obstinate in one’s position in opposition and resistance to the truth. عَنِيدٍ (‘Aneed) refers to the person who stubbornly opposes the truth.
Derivative: جَبَّارٍ (Jabbaar) refers to someone who has the power to dominate over and oppress i.e., a tyrant.

Derivative: اسْتَعْمَرَ means to settle for a long time. It comes from عُمْر (‘Umr) which refers to a lifespan or age. اسْتَعْمَرَ implies to settle for ages, one life span after another i.e., for generations.

Derivative: مَرْجُوًّا (Marjuw) literally means “To be hoped for” and here it means “a source of hope” from رَجَاء (Rajaa’) which means “hope”. Here it is being used to say: “You were a source of hope amongst us” i.e., that you would achieve great things in life.

Derivative: عَقَرُو is from عَقَرَ – يَعْقِرُ – عَقْر which means to inflict a wound and is used to refer to the act of hamstringing an animal by cutting the tendons behind the knee to make it come down which is followed by slaughtering it. It can also generally be used to refer to the act of slaughtering an animal.
- كَفَرَ carries the meanings of both disbelief and ingratitude. When it is followed by بِ (Bi) it implies “to disbelieve in” something. Without بِ (Bi) it implies the meaning “to be ungrateful”.
Derivative: أَوْجَسَ means to have a sense of dread about something. It is a fear and apprehension that is hidden but felt or sensed.
Derivative: خِيفَةً (Kheefah) comes from خَافَ – يَخُوفُ – خَوْف which means “to fear”. خِيفَةً (Kheefah) is “an instance of fear” which comes as a result of sensing something.
Derivative: الرَّوْعُ comes from رَاعَ – يَرُوعُ – رَوْعًا which means to be frightened with an implication of being startled, shocked and alarmed.
Derivative: حَلِيمٌ (Haleem) carries the meaning of being forbearing, patient and tolerating.

Derivative: أَوَّاهٌ comes from آهَ – يَأُوهُ and refers to the sound that is made from the outpouring of emotions. It can refer to the sounds of emotion whilst engaged in supplication and prayer, like the sounds of sobbing. It is also used to refer to being soft hearted and compassionate. أَوَّاهٌ is the hyperbolised form (صِيغَةُ المُبَالَغة – Seeghat ul-Mubaalaghah) and can indicate that he was extremely emotional in his supplications or extremely soft hearted and compassionate in general as a characteristic.
Derivative: عَصِيبٌ (‘Aseeb) comes from عَصَبَ which contains the meanings of severity and pressure. يَوْمٌ عَصِيبٌ (Yawmun ‘Aseebun) can be understood to mean “A really tough or testing day”.

- ضَاقَ بِهِمْ ذَرْعًا literally means “All capability was lost in respect to them”. Here it means that “He felt great unease in respect to them”. ضَاقَ ذَرْعًا عَن is an expression that means for ones capabilities to become constricted i.e., when a matter becomes overwhelming or unbearable.

Derivative: يَحْبِسُ comes from حَبَسَ – حَبْس and it means to “detain”, “confine” or “to hold back” someone or something. It can combine the meanings of being detained and confined or constrained at the same time.

Derivative: أَلَا (Alaa) is used for the purpose of grabbing attention to the statement that follows it in Arabic. It could be understood to mean “Listen up”. It has also been interpreted to imply the meaning of “unquestioningly” or “Without doubt” similar to إِنَّ (Inna).
Derivative: أُمَّةٍ (Ummah) has numerous meanings such as “a nation” and “a Deen”. Its meaning here is a “time span” or “period of time” at the end of which something is inevitably going to happen.
Derivative: حَاقَ – يَحِيقُ can mean to fall or descend upon someone, like a punishment. It can also mean to surround and beset from all sides.
Derivative: رُكْن (Rukn) refers to a strong part or section of something like a house or mountain that supports it and props it up. It refers here to a source or place of support.
Derivative: أَسْرِ is the command form from أَسْرَى – يُسْرِي – أِسْرَاء which means “to travel by/at night”.

Derivative: مَّنضُودٍ comes from نَضَدَ – يَنْضِدُ – نَضْدًا which means “to line up” where one thing follows another. مَّنضُودٍ (Mandood) means “lined up” and implies consecutiveness where the stones of baked clay (حِجَارَةً مِّن سِجِّيلٍ) come down one following the other (مَّنضُودٍ).

- Literally means: “We made (جَعَلْنَا) its high (عَالِيَهَا) its low (سَافِلَهَا)”. Its high can refer to the roofs of their homes which were collapsed to the bottom of the ground. It is an expression that means “to turn completely upside down” which in turn implies to “bring complete destruction”.

Derivative: مُّسَوَّمَةً (Musawwamah) means “to be marked” from the verb سَوَّمَ which means “to provide with a mark”. There are different views regarding what the stones were marked with. One view is that the stones were marked with the names of each of their targets.

Derivative: يَئُوسٌ comes from يَئِسَ – يَئِسَ – يَأْس which means to be in a state of despair, despondency and feeling hopeless and desperate. يَائِس (Yaa’is) is the person in the state of despair and يَئُوسٌ (Ya’oos) is the hyperbolised and accentuated form meaning: “Extremely despairing, despondent or desperate”.
Derivative: نَزَعْنَا comes from نَزَعَ – يَنْزِعُ – نَزْعًا which means to “pull or tear” something out from its place that it had been settled in.

Derivative: رَهْطُ (Raht) can refer to a small group (less than ten) of men excluding women. It can also refer to the clan and wider family of a person or a group that a person belongs to. Here it refers to a group that acts as protection and backup.

Derivative: رَقِيبٌ (Raqeeb) is from رَقَبَ – يَرْقُبُ – رَقَابَة which means to watch and observe something attentively that one has control over in contrast to the verb اِرْتَقَبَ from the same root which relates to watching out for something that is not under your control.
Derivative: ارْتَقِبُوا comes from اِرْتَقَبَ which carries the meaning of “to wait and watch in anticipation” of something that is going to happen.
Derivative: الْوِرْدُ (Al-Wird) is the watering place where animals go for water.
Derivative: أَوْرَدَ means “to lead someone or an animal to water”. In general, it is also used to mean “to make something arrive to a place”.
Derivative: الرِّفْدُ (Ar-Rifd) refers to a gift or assistance that is given to someone to help him undertake his interests or needs.
Derivative: الْمَرْفُودُ (Al-Marfood) is that which has been given as a gift or assistance.
